name: Abū Ḥanīfah al-Nuʿmān ibn Thābit
bio: Born in Kūfah circa 80 AH (699 CE) to a Persian silk merchant, Abū Ḥanīfah is the eponymous founder of the Ḥanafī school — though he himself never claimed to establish a madhhab. He studied under the great Kūfan jurist Ḥammād ibn Abī Sulaymān for eighteen years and also learned from scholars in the ḥadīth-rich circles of Basrah and Makkah. Known for his rigorous dialectical method (raʾy) and his principle that legal opinion should serve human welfare, he compiled Kitāb al-Āthār, transmitting the precedent of his teachers. Twice offered the position of qāḍī by the ʿAbbāsids, he refused and was imprisoned; he died in custody in 150 AH (767 CE). His two foremost students, Abū Yūsuf and al-Shaybānī, would codify and systematise his legal reasoning into the structured corpus that became the Ḥanafī madhhab.
note: Founder of the Ḥanafī school

name: Abū Yūsuf Yaʿqūb ibn Ibrāhīm
bio: Born in Kūfah in 113 AH (731 CE), Abū Yūsuf was Abū Ḥanīfah's most senior student and the first Ḥanafī to hold the office of Qāḍī al-Quḍāt (Chief Judge) under the ʿAbbāsid caliph Hārūn al-Rashīd. His position gave the Ḥanafī school its first foothold in state apparatus. His Kitāb al-Kharāj is a landmark treatise on public finance and taxation — one of the earliest systematic works on Islamic fiscal law — while his Al-Amālī preserved the legal opinions of Abū Ḥanīfah. He served as a bridge between the school's intellectual origins and its institutional establishment, dying in 182 AH (798 CE).

name: Muḥammad ibn al-Ḥasan al-Shaybānī
bio: Born in Wasit in 132 AH (749 CE) and raised in Kūfah, al-Shaybānī was the second great disciple of Abū Ḥanīfah and the true codifier of Ḥanafī law. He also studied under Mālik ibn Anas in Madīnah, gaining expertise in ḥadīth that Abū Ḥanīfah's Kūfan circle lacked. His authorship of al-Aṣl, al-Jāmiʿ al-Ṣaghīr, al-Jāmiʿ al-Kabīr, al-Ziyādāt, and al-Siyar al-Kabīr forms the core textual corpus (zāhir al-riwāyah) upon which all later Ḥanafī scholarship is built. He served as a judge in Raqqa and accompanied Hārūn al-Rashīd's campaigns. Died in 189 AH (805 CE).
works: Al-Aṣl, al-Jāmiʿ al-Ṣaghīr/al-Kabīr, al-Ziyādāt, al-Siyar al-Kabīr

name: Zufr ibn al-Ḥudhayl
bio: A leading student of Imam Abū Ḥanīfa and one of the foremost early jurists of the Hanafi school from Isfahan. Renowned for his exceptional skill in analogical reasoning (qiyās), he is frequently counted as one of the most authoritative transmitters of the school's founder, earning him the title

name: al-Ḥasan ibn Ziyād al-Luʾluʾī
bio: A prominent student of Abū Ḥanīfa and leading jurist of Kūfa, known for his work *al-Mujarrad*. He was one of the key early transmitters of the school's teachings in Iraq and his legal opinions were considered highly authoritative. His contribution to the systematization of Hanafi fiqh was foundational for the school's development.

name: Abū Sulaymān Mūsā al-Jūzajānī
bio: A student of Muḥammad al-Shaybānī and Abū Yūsuf, he was a respected Hanafi scholar of Transoxiana and Khurāsān. His compilation *Nawādir Abī Sulaymān* preserved important legal opinions of the school's early authorities. He was instrumental in transmitting Hanafi teachings to the eastern Islamic lands.

name: Muʿallā ibn Manṣūr al-Rāzī
bio: A Hanafi jurist and hadith scholar from Rayy, known for his work *Nawādir Muʿallā ibn Manṣūr*. A contemporary of al-Shaybānī, he transmitted the school's teachings in the Jibāl region and was recognized for his expertise in both fiqh and hadith sciences. His opinions are cited in later Hanafi literature.

name: Abū Ḥafṣ al-Kabīr al-Bukhārī
bio: A major Hanafi scholar from Bukhara in Transoxiana, he was one of the principal early transmitters of the school in Central Asia. Known as

name: ʿĪsā ibn Abān
bio: A student of Muḥammad al-Shaybānī, he was an important early Hanafi jurist known for his works *al-Ḥujaj al-Ṣaghīr* and *al-Ḥujaj al-Kabīr*. His contributions to the principles of Hanafi jurisprudence (uṣūl) are frequently referenced by later scholars such as al-Jaṣṣāṣ. He was recognized for his expertise in legal reasoning and hadith.

name: Muḥammad ibn Samāʿah al-Tamīmī
bio: A student of Abū Yūsuf and a prominent jurist of Baghdad, he was a leading Hanafi authority in Iraq during his era. He authored *Kitāb Adab al-Qāḍī*, a foundational work on the ethics and procedure of the judicial office. His legal opinions were widely transmitted and remained influential in the school.

name: Aḥmad ibn ʿAmr al-Khaṣṣāf
bio: A prominent Hanafi jurist of Baghdad, known as

name: Muḥammad ibn Shujāʿ al-Thaljī
bio: A leading Hanafi jurist of Baghdad, known for his work *Kitāb al-Manāsik* on the rituals of Hajj. He was one of the prominent scholars of the Iraqi Hanafi tradition during the 3rd century AH. His writings contributed to the codification of Hanafi ritual law.

name: Abū Jaʿfar Aḥmad al-Ṭaḥāwī
bio: Born in Ṭaḥā (Egypt) in 239 AH (853 CE), al-Ṭaḥāwī is one of the most revered scholars in Islamic history. Initially a Shāfiʿī under his maternal uncle al-Muzanī, he switched to the Ḥanafī madhhab and became its greatest Egyptian exponent. His Sharḥ Maʿānī al-Āthār is a masterwork of ḥadīth-based jurisprudence, while al-Mukhtaṣar remains one of the most widely-studied Ḥanafī furūʿ texts ever written. He bridged the gap between the ḥadīth-oriented and raʾy-oriented approaches, earning respect across all schools. Died 321 AH (933 CE).
note: Master of ḥadīth and fiqh

name: Abū Manṣūr al-Māturīdī
bio: Born in Samarqand, Abū Manṣūr al-Māturīdī (d. 333 AH / 944 CE) is the founder of the Māturīdī theological school, one of the two mainstream Sunnī kalām traditions (alongside Ashʿarism). His Kitāb al-Tawḥīd is a systematic work of Islamic theology defending Sunnī doctrine against the Muʿtazilah, while his Taʾwīlāt Ahl al-Sunnah provides a Ḥanafī approach to Qurʾānic exegesis. The Māturīdī creed became the dominant theological position of the Ḥanafī world, particularly in Transoxiana, Turkey, and South Asia.
note: Founder of Māturīdī theology

name: Muḥammad al-Ḥākim al-Shahīd
bio: A distinguished Hanafi jurist from Merv who served as qāḍī in Bukhara and as vizier to the Sāmānids. He was given the epithet

name: Abū al-Ḥasan al-Karkhī
bio: A leading Hanafi jurist of Baghdad, renowned for his contributions to legal theory and his compilation of foundational legal maxims (*qawāʿid fiqhiyyah*), known as *Uṣūl al-Karkhī*. His work on general principles of fiqh became a cornerstone for later Hanafi scholarship. He was considered one of the most important jurists of his generation in Iraq.

name: Abū Bakr al-Jaṣṣāṣ al-Rāzī
bio: Born in 305 AH (917 CE), al-Jaṣṣāṣ was a towering figure of Ḥanafī uṣūl al-fiqh. His Aḥkām al-Qurʾān is one of the great works of Qurʾānic legal exegesis, while his Sharḥ Mukhtaṣar al-Ṭaḥāwī established a template for Ḥanafī commentary literature. He systematised the principles of legal reasoning that would underpin all subsequent Ḥanafī scholarship. Died 370 AH (980 CE).

name: Abū Jaʿfar al-Hiduwānī
bio: A Hanafi jurist of the 4th century AH, recognized as one of the transmitters of the school's teachings in the generation following the early founders. His scholarly work contributed to the preservation and transmission of Hanafi legal doctrine. He was part of the scholarly network that sustained the school's development in the eastern Islamic world.

name: Abū al-Layth al-Samarqandī (al-Faqīh)
bio: A major Hanafi jurist and exegete from Samarqand, known as

name: Abū al-Ḥusayn Aḥmad al-Qudūrī
bio: Born in 362 AH (972 CE), al-Qudūrī's Mukhtaṣar is perhaps the single most widely-memorised Ḥanafī furūʿ text — the first book many students of the madhhab study after Arabic grammar. Its concise yet comprehensive treatment of ritual and transactional law made it the backbone of Ḥanafī education for a millennium. He also authored al-Tajrīd, a more detailed compendium. Died 428 AH (1037 CE).

name: Abū Zayd ʿUbayd Allāh al-Dabūsī
bio: A central figure in Ḥanafī uṣūl al-fiqh, al-Dabūsī's Taqwīm al-Adillah was the first systematic work to establish legal-theoretical principles for the Ḥanafī school. He introduced the concept of takhṣīṣ (specification) and developed the theory of ʿillah (legal cause) in ways that influenced all later uṣūl works. Born 367 AH, died 430 AH (1038 CE).

name: Abū al-ʿAbbās Aḥmad al-Nāṭifī
bio: A Hanafi jurist from Jurjān (Nāṭif), known for his contributions to fiqh and legal theory. He was part of the generation of scholars who consolidated the Hanafi school's teachings in the eastern regions. His scholarly works were referenced in the later Hanafi legal tradition.

name: Shams al-Aʾimmah al-Ḥalwānī
bio: A towering Hanafi jurist of Bukhara, given the honorific

name: Abū al-Ḥasan ʿAlī al-Sughdī
bio: A Hanafi jurist from Sughd in Transoxiana, recognized as an important scholar in the Central Asian tradition. He contributed to the transmission and development of Hanafi legal doctrine in the region during the 5th century AH. His scholarly lineage connected him to the major jurists of Bukhara and Samarqand.

name: Abū Naṣr Aḥmad al-Aqṭaʿ
bio: A student of the famous Hanafi jurist al-Qudūrī, he was known for his commentary on *Mukhtaṣar al-Qudūrī*. He was an important figure in the 5th-century Hanafi scholarly tradition, contributing to the study and teaching of the school's concise legal manuals. His work aided the popularization of Hanafi fiqh.

name: al-Qāḍī Aḥmad ibn Manṣūr al-Isbījābī
bio: A Hanafi qāḍī and scholar from Isbījāb, known for his commentary on *Mukhtaṣar al-Ṭaḥāwī*. He served as a judge and was recognized for his expertise in both fiqh and legal procedure. His writings contributed to the judicial application of Hanafi law in Central Asia.

name: Fakhr al-Islām ʿAlī al-Bazdawī
bio: A leading Hanafi jurist from Bazdāw (near Nasaf), renowned for his work in uṣūl al-fiqh. He authored *Uṣūl al-Bazdawī* (also known as *Kanz al-Wuṣūl*), which became a foundational text for the study of Hanafi legal theory. He was a key figure in the intellectual flourishing of the school in the 5th century.

name: Shaykh al-Islām Khuwāhar Zādah
bio: A Hanafi jurist of the 5th century AH, recognized for his scholarly contributions to the school. He was part of the Central Asian Hanafi scholarly elite, and his legal opinions were considered authoritative in the Transoxianan tradition. His name appears in the major Hanafi biographical and chronological lists.

name: Shams al-Aʾimmah al-Sarakhsī
bio: One of the most important Ḥanafī scholars of all time. Born in Saras, Transoxiana, he was imprisoned by the Qarakhanid ruler for defending a legal opinion that challenged state policy. During his five-year imprisonment, he composed from memory his monumental Al-Mabsūṭ — a 30-volume commentary on al-Shaybānī's works that became the standard reference for Ḥanafī substantive law. His Sharḥ al-Siyar al-Kabīr is equally foundational for international law (siyar). Died circa 490 AH (1096 CE).
note: Author of the 30-volume Al-Mabsūṭ

name: Zahīr al-Dīn al-Marghīnānī al-Kabīr
bio: A Hanafi jurist from Marghīnān in the Farghāna Valley, recognized as an early scholarly figure in the region before the fame of Burhan al-Dīn al-Marghīnānī. He contributed to the Hanafi legal tradition of Transoxiana. His works represent the continuity of Hanafi scholarship in the area.

name: Shaykh al-Islām ʿAlī al-Isbījābī
bio: A prominent Hanafi scholar from Isbījāb in Central Asia, honored with the title

name: Ḥusām al-Dīn al-Ṣadr al-Shahīd
bio: A major Hanafi jurist from the ʿAlī Mūzā family, known as

name: Najm al-Dīn ʿUmar al-Nasafī
bio: A towering Hanafi scholar from Nasaf, known as

name: ʿAlāʾ al-Dīn Muḥammad al-Samarqandī
bio: A leading Hanafi jurist of Central Asia, teacher of the famous ʿAlāʾ al-Dīn al-Kāsānī, who later married his daughter. He authored *Tuḥfat al-Fuqahāʾ*, an important foundational text in Hanafi jurisprudence. His scholarly circle in Samarqand was one of the most prominent of the 6th century.

name: Jār Allāh Maḥmūd al-Zamakhsharī
bio: Born 1074 in Zamakhshar, Khwārizm, al-Zamakhsharī was one of the greatest Arabic linguists and grammarians of the medieval period. A Muʿtazilī rationalist and Ḥanafī jurist, he traveled extensively through Bukhārā, Rayy, Iṣfahān, and Baghdād before settling in Mecca for five years — earning him the epithet Jār Allāh (Neighbour of God). His greatest work, al-Kashshāf, is the cornerstone of Quranic linguistic exegesis, renowned for its elegant Arabic style and interrogative pedagogical method. His al-Mufaṣṣal is considered by grammarians the second most important syntactic work after Ṣibawayh. He was a fierce proponent of the Muʿtazilī rationalist tradition and was noted for his independent approach to grammar, drawing from both the Baṣran and Kūfan schools as evidence demanded. Died 1143 in Jurjāniyyah, Khwārizm.
works: Al-Kashshāf ʿanḤaqāʾiq al-Tanzīl, al-Mufaṣṣal fī Ṣināʿat al-Iʿrāb
note: One of the greatest Arabic grammarians in history; author of al-Kashshāf

name: Abū al-Fath ʿAbd al-Rashīd al-Walwālijī
bio: A Hanafi jurist devoted to the study and transmission of Hanafi jurisprudence in the 6th century. He was part of the scholarly network that preserved the school's teachings in the eastern Islamic lands. His works contributed to the consolidation of Hanafi legal doctrine.

name: Aḥmad ibn Mūsā al-Kashshī
bio: A scholar from Kashsh in Transoxiana, known for his expertise in hadith and genealogical studies. His ascription to the region of Kashsh places him within the Central Asian scholarly tradition. His contributions included the evaluation of hadith transmitters and regional history.

name: Nāṣir al-Dīn Muḥammad al-Samarqandī
bio: A Hanafi jurist of Samarqand in the 6th century, known for his contributions to the legal tradition of the school. He was part of the scholarly lineage that sustained the Hanafi school in Central Asia during a period of intellectual flourishing. His works were studied in the madrasas of the region.

name: Rukn al-Dīn al-Kirānī
bio: A Hanafi scholar of the 6th century, known for his contributions to the jurisprudential and theological discourse of the school. He was part of the generation of scholars who continued the legacy of the Central Asian Hanafi tradition. His scholarly work is referenced in the later Hanafi literature.

name: Sirāj al-Dīn ʿAlī al-Ūshī
bio: A prominent Hanafi-Māturīdī scholar from Ūsh in the Farghāna Valley. He authored the famous *al-Qaṣīdah al-Lāmiyyah fī al-Tawḥīd* (known as *Badʾ al-Amālī*) and *al-Fatāwā al-Sirājiyyah*, which became standard texts in Islamic education. His theological poem was widely memorized and commented upon.

name: Abū al-Muẓaffar Asʿad al-Karābīsī
bio: A Hanafi scholar of the 6th century, recognized for his contributions to the legal and scholarly traditions of the school. He was part of the Central Asian scholarly community that preserved and transmitted Hanafi fiqh. His scholarly work is noted in the major Hanafi biographical lists.

name: Raḍī al-Dīn Muḥammad al-Sarakhsī
bio: A Hanafi scholar of the 6th century, from the region of Sarakhs. He represents the continuation of the Hanafi tradition in the lands of Khurāsān and Transoxiana. His contributions to fiqh and the transmission of the school's teachings are recognized in the biographical sources.

name: Abū Naṣr Aḥmad al-ʿAṭṭābī
bio: A Hanafi jurist known for his commentary on *Badāʾiʿ al-Ṣanāʾiʿ* of al-Kāsānī. He was an important scholar of the late 6th century who contributed to the exegesis of Hanafi legal texts. His work aided in the teaching and understanding of complex Hanafi rulings.

name: Abū Bakr ibn Masʿūd al-Kāsānī
bio: Born in Kāsān, Transoxiana, al-Kāsānī was a student of al-Samarqandī and married his teacher's daughter. His Badāʾiʿ al-Ṣanāʾiʿ fī Tartīb al-Sharāʾiʿ is universally regarded as one of the greatest works of Islamic jurisprudence — a massive, encyclopaedic commentary on al-Samarqandī's Tuḥfat al-Fuqahāʾ covering every branch of fiqh with unmatched analytical depth and elegance. Died 587 AH (1191 CE).

name: Ḥasan ibn Manṣūr QāḍīKhān
bio: Leading Hanafi jurist and chief judge (qāḍī) from Farghāna, Transoxiana. He authored the monumental *Fatāwā Qāḍīkhān*, a comprehensive multi-volume collection of Hanafi legal opinions that became a primary reference for muftis and courts. He also wrote *ʿUmdat al-Muftī* and *Sharḥ al-Jāmiʿ al-Ṣaghīr*.

name: ʿAlī ibn Abī Bakr al-Marghīnānī
bio: Born in Marghīnān, Transoxiana, al-Marghīnānī is the author of Al-Hidāyah — arguably the most celebrated and widely-studied Ḥanafī text ever written. Composed as a commentary on his own al-Tajnīs, Al-Hidāyah systematises the entire field of Ḥanafī law and became the subject of more supercommentaries than any other work in the madhhab. It remains the core textbook in Ḥanafī madrasas worldwide. Died 593 AH (1197 CE).
note: Author of Al-Hidāyah, the most celebrated Ḥanafī text

name: Jamāl al-Dīn Aḥmad al-Ghaznawī
bio: Hanafi jurist, theologian, and Kalām scholar of the Maturīdī school from Ghazna. He studied under ʿAlāʾ al-Dīn al-Kāsānī and authored *Kitāb Uṣūl al-Dīn* and *Al-Ḥāwī al-Qudsī fī Furūʿ al-Fiqh al-Ḥanafī*, written in Jerusalem. He died in Aleppo, Syria.

name: Ḥusām al-Dīn ʿAlī al-Rāzī
bio: Hanafi jurist from Rayy, Persia, known for his contributions to the school's legal methodology in the late 12th century. He was part of the Central Asian-Persian Hanafi tradition that emphasized legal reasoning and systematic jurisprudence.

name: Ṭāhir ibn Aḥmad al-Bukhārī
bio: Hanafi scholar from Bukhārā in Transoxiana, active in the late 6th and early 7th centuries AH. He contributed to the transmission and teaching of Hanafi doctrine in Central Asia.

name: Burhān al-Dīn Maḥmūd al-Bukhārī
bio: Distinguished Hanafi jurist from Bukhārā, known as Ibn Māza al-Bukhārī. He authored *Al-Muḥīṭ al-Burhānī fī al-Fiqh al-Nuʿmānī* and *Al-Dhakhīra al-Burhāniyya*, major works in Hanafi jurisprudence that influenced subsequent legal discourse.

name: Zahīr al-Dīn Muḥammad al-Bukhārī
bio: Hanafi scholar from Bukhārā, Transoxiana, active in the early 13th century. He contributed to the scholarly environment of Bukhārā, a major Hanafi intellectual center.

name: Majd al-Dīn Muḥammad al-Uṣrūshnī
bio: Hanafi jurist from Uṣrūshna (Ushrusana) in Transoxiana. He was part of the Central Asian Hanafi tradition that produced many leading jurists during the 7th century AH.

name: Shams al-Aʾimmah al-Kardarī
bio: Hanafi scholar from Kardar in Transoxiana, known for his expertise in fiqh and kalām. He authored *Taʾsīs al-Qawāʿid* and wrote a *Risāla fī Radd ʿalā al-Mankhūl al-Ghazālī*, contributing to the doctrinal defense of the Hanafi school.

name: ʿAbd al-Raḥīm Ḥafīd Ṣāḥib al-Hidāyah
bio: Hanafi scholar known as Ḥafīḍ al-Hidāyah for his mastery and transmission of al-Hidāyah, the foundational Hanafi legal text. He was active in the mid-7th century, contributing to the standardization of Hanafi legal education.

name: ʿAlāʾ al-Dīn al-Tarjumānī
bio: Hanafi scholar of the mid-7th century, known for his scholarly contributions to the Hanafi school in the eastern Islamic lands.

name: Najm al-Dīn Mukhtār al-Zāhidī
bio: Hanafi scholar from Ghazmīn (al-Zāhidī). He authored *Al-Mujtabā Sharḥ Mukhtasar al-Qudūrī*, a major 5-volume commentary on al-Qudūrī's primer in Hanafi fiqh.

name: Zayn al-Dīn Muḥammad al-Rāzī
bio: Hanafi jurist from Rayy, Persia, active in the mid-7th century AH. He contributed to the Persian branch of Hanafi legal scholarship.

name: Tāj al-Sharīʿah Maḥmūd ibn Aḥmad
bio: From the distinguished Maḥbūbī family of Hanafi scholars in Bukhārā. He was the grandfather of the famous Sadr al-Sharīʿah and contributed to the family's legacy of Hanafi jurisprudence in Transoxiana.

name: Jalāl al-Dīn al-Karlānī
bio: Hanafi scholar of the late 7th century, contributing to the school's development in the eastern regions of the Islamic world.

name: Abū al-Faḍl ʿAbd Allāh al-Mawṣilī
bio: Prominent Hanafi jurist and muḥaddith from Mosul, Iraq (born 599 AH). He authored the reliable Hanafi matn *Al-Mukhtār* and its commentary *Al-Ikhtiyār*, which became standard texts in the Hanafi curriculum. He served as a judge in Kūfa and taught in Baghdad.

name: Muẓaffar al-Dīn Aḥmad Ibn al-Sāʿātī
bio: Hanafi scholar known for his expertise in legal theory. He authored *Sharḥ Majmūʿ al-Bahrayn wa Multaqā al-Nayrayn*, one of the four important Hanafi mutūn (principles) alongside *Al-Mukhtār*, *Al-Kanz*, and *Al-Wiqāyah*.

name: ʿUmar ibn Muḥammad al-Sunnāmī
bio: Hanafi jurist active in the late 7th and early 8th centuries. He contributed to the transmission of Hanafi legal teachings.

name: Dāwūd ibn Yūsuf al-Khaṭīb
bio: Hanafi scholar and intellectual of the 8th century, known for his contributions to jurisprudence and theology during the Islamic Golden Age.

name: Muḥammad ibn Muḥammad al-Kāshgharī
bio: Hanafi scholar from Kāshghar in the eastern Islamic world. He authored *Munyat al-Muṣallī wa-Ghunyat al-Mubtadiʾ*, a recognized Hanafi work on prayer and worship.

name: Ḥāfiẓ al-Dīn Abū al-Barakāt al-Nasafī
bio: Born circa 620 AH (1223 CE) in Nasaf, he produced three of the most widely-studied Ḥanafī texts: Al-Manār (uṣūl al-fiqh), Kanz al-Daqāʾiq (furūʿ — an abridgement of his own al-Wāfī), and al-Kāfī (supercommentary). Together these works formed a pedagogical triad covering legal theory, positive law, and commentary — a pattern replicated across Ḥanafī madrasa curricula. Died 710 AH (1310 CE).

name: Abū al-ʿAbbās Aḥmad al-Sarūjī
bio: Hanafi jurist and Chief Justice (Qāḍī al-Quḍāh) in Egypt. He authored *Al-Ghāyah fī Sharḥ al-Hidāyah*, a rare 14-volume commentary on al-Hidāyah notable for its discussion of hadith chains and inclusion of opinions from other schools. He was originally a Ḥanbalī before becoming Hanafi.

name: Ḥusām al-Dīn Ḥusayn al-Sighnāqī
bio: Prominent Hanafi jurist from Sighnāq (modern Kazakhstan), expert in fiqh, uṣūl al-fiqh, and Arabic linguistics. He laid solid foundations for Islamic scholarly traditions in Central Asia and the Golden Horde, and his works were preserved and studied by later generations.

name: Fakhr al-Dīn ʿUthmān al-Zaylaʿī
bio: Author of Tabyīn al-Ḥaqāʾiq, one of the four Muʿtamad (reliable) Ḥanafī works and a massive commentary on al-Qudūrī and al-Kāsānī. Al-Zaylaʿī was a leading Egyptian Ḥanafī scholar. Died 743 AH (1342 CE).

name: Ṣadr al-Sharīʿah ʿUbayd Allāh ibn Maḥmūd
bio: Major Hanafi jurist, theologian, and logician from the Maḥbūbī family of Bukhārā, known as Sadr al-Sharīʿah al-Thānī. His family migrated to Kirmān due to Mongol invasions; he later settled in Herāt and Sharʿābād. He authored *Wiqāyat al-Riwāyah* and became a pillar of the madrasa curriculum.

name: Qiwām al-Dīn Muḥammad al-Kākī
bio: Hanafi scholar from the Kākī family, active in the mid-8th century. He contributed to the Hanafi legal tradition in the post-Mongol period.

name: Amīr Kātib al-Itqānī
bio: Hanafi scholar known as a famous explainer of al-Hidāyah. He traveled to Damascus in 747 AH and was known for his expertise in Hanafi legal methodology. He is also referred to as Qiwām al-Dīn Amīr Kātib ibn Amīr ʿUmar al-Fārābī al-Iṭqānī.

name: Ibrāhīm ibn ʿAlī al-Tarasūsī
bio: Hanafi scholar from Tarsus (Tarāʿūs) in Anatolia. He contributed to the spread of Hanafi scholarship in the regions that would later become part of the Ottoman Empire.

name: Jamāl al-Dīn ʿAbd Allāh al-Zaylaʿī
bio: Hanafi hadith master (muḥaddith) from Zaylaʿ (Somalia), born 672 AH. He authored *Naṣb al-Rāyah li Aḥādīth al-Hidāyah*, the definitive reference for the hadith evidence behind the Hanafi school's rulings. He died and was buried in Cairo.

name: ʿAbd al-Wahhāb ibn Aḥmad Ibn Wahbān
bio: Hanafi scholar and hadith expert from the Mizzī family. He authored a comprehensive commentary on *Tajrīd Asmāʾ al-Ṣaḥābah*, contributing to the intersection of Hanafi fiqh and hadith studies.

name: Ṭāhir ibn Islām al-Khāwrizmī
bio: Hanafi scholar from Khwārizm in Central Asia, active in the mid-8th century. He contributed to the Hanafi legal tradition in the region.

name: Sirāj al-Dīn ʿUmar al-Ghaznawī al-Hindī
bio: Hanafi scholar, theologian, and Kalām scholar of the Maturīdī school from Ghazna (born ~704 AH). He served as Qāḍī al-Quḍāh and authored a renowned commentary on *al-ʿAqīda al-Ṭaḥāwiyya* that remains highly recommended for students. He died in Cairo.

name: ʿĀlim ibn al-ʿAlāʾ al-Andarpātī
bio: Hanafi scholar of the 8th century, contributing to the school's scholarly development in the eastern Islamic world.

name: Akmal al-Dīn Muḥammad al-Bābartī
bio: Hanafi scholar from Bābirt (Bābartī) in Anatolia, born 710 AH. He was a prominent Hanafi jurist who wrote important works on Hanafi fiqh and hadith, and was known as a major scholar of his era in the Ottoman lands.

name: Shams al-Dīn Muḥammad al-Qūnawī
bio: Hanafi scholar from Qūnaw (Qonya/Konya) in Anatolia. He contributed to the Hanafi intellectual tradition in the region before the Ottoman rise.

name: Abū Bakr ibn ʿAlī al-Ḥaddād
bio: Yemeni Hanafi jurist from Zubayd, known as Abū Bakr ibn ʿAlī al-Ḥaddād al-Zubaydī al-Yamanī. He contributed to the spread of Hanafi jurisprudence in Yemen.

name: ʿAbd al-Laṭīf Ibn Malak
bio: Hanafi scholar (ʿAbd al-Laṭīf ibn ʿAbd al-ʿAzīz), known for his commentary *Sharḥ al-Wiqāyah*. He was a jurist and hadith scholar who died in 801 AH (1398 CE).

name: Maḥmūd ibn Isrāʾīl Ibn Qāḍī Samāwna
bio: Also known as Badr al-Dīn ibn Qāḍī Samāwna or Sheikh Bedreddin. Born in Simāwna (Ottoman Rumelia), he was a philosopher, theologian, and Hanafi jurist. He authored *Jāmiʿ al-Fuṣūlīn* and *Ḥaqīqa al-Ḥaqāʾiq*.

name: Muḥammad al-Bazzāzī al-Kardarī
bio: Hanafi jurist known as Ibn al-Bazzāzī or Ḥāfiẓ al-Dīn al-Kardarī al-Khwārizmī. He authored *Al-Fatāwā al-Bazzāziyya*, an important collection of Hanafi legal rulings. He died in 827 AH.

name: Sirāj al-Dīn Abū Ḥafṣ ʿUmar Qārīʾ al-Hidāyah
bio: Distinguished Hanafi scholar from Cairo, known as Qārīʾ al-Hidāyah. Full name: Sirāj al-Dīn Abū Ḥafṣ ʿUmar ibn ʿAlī al-Kināni al-Qāhirī al-Ḥusaynī. He was an expert in Hanafi jurisprudence and Quranic recitation.

name: Abū al-Ḥasan ʿAlī al-Ṭarābulusī
bio: Distinguished Hanafi scholar from Tripoli (Ṭarābulus). He authored *Muʿīn al-Ḥukkām fīmā Yatardad bayna al-Khaṣmayn min al-Aḥkām*, an important work on judicial procedure and legal rulings.

name: Abū al-Baqāʾ Muḥammad Ibn al-Diyāʾ
bio: Hanafi jurist from Mecca, born 789 AH (Bahāʾ al-Dīn Abū al-Baqāʾ Muḥammad ibn Aḥmad al-Qurashī al-ʿUmarī al-Makkī). He served as Qāḍī al-Quḍāh and Muḥtasib of Mecca, and authored *Al-Ḍiyāʾ al-Maʿnawī Sharḥ Muqaddimat al-Ghaznawī* and works on tafsīr and fiqh.

name: Badr al-Dīn Maḥmūd al-ʿAynī
bio: Born in ʿAyntāb (Gaziantep) in 762 AH (1360 CE), al-ʿAynī was a polymath who produced landmark works across multiple disciplines: Al-Bināyah (Ḥanafī fiqh commentary on al-Hidāyah), Ramz al-Ḥaqāʾiq, and his monumental ʿUmdat al-Qārī — one of the two great commentaries on Ṣaḥīḥ al-Bukhārī. He served as chief Ḥanafī judge and chief mufti in Cairo and was known for his encyclopaedic learning. Died 855 AH (1451 CE).

name: Kamāl al-Dīn Ibn al-Humām
bio: Born in Alexandria in 788 AH (1386 CE), Ibn al-Humām is widely regarded as one of the greatest Ḥanafī commentators and mujtahids. His Fatḥ al-Qadīr — a supercommentary on al-Hidāyah — is the single most authoritative Ḥanafī commentary, combining legal analysis with ḥadīth verification. His Zād al-Faqīr covers almost every area of fiqh in a single volume. He was also a ḥadīth specialist and Shāfiʿī-trained before becoming Ḥanafī. Died 861 AH (1457 CE).
note: One of the greatest Ḥanafī commentators

name: Abū al-Fidāʾ Qāsim ibn Qutlūbughā
bio: Hanafi jurist and scholar known for his works in legal methodology and hadith. He authored *Tashīḥ wa al-Tarjīḥ ʿalā Mukhtasar al-Qudūrī* and *Munyat al-Almaʾī fīmā Fāta min Takhrij Aḥādīth al-Hidāyah li al-Zaylaʿī*.

name: Shams al-Dīn Ibn Amīr Hājj
bio: Hanafi scholar from Aleppo, born 823 AH. He authored *Al-Taqrīr wa al-Taḥbīr*, an important commentary on Hanafi uṣūl al-fiqh, and was a major jurist of the Mamluk/Ottoman transition period.

name: Muḥammad ibn Farāmurz Mullā Khusraw
bio: Leading Hanafi jurist of the Ottoman era, born in Istanbul. He authored *Durar al-Ḥukkām fī Sharḥ Ghurar al-Aḥkām* (a major legal text) and *Mirʾāt al-Uṣūl* (on uṣūl al-fiqh). He served as Shaykh al-Islām for approximately 20 years and was buried in Bursa.

name: Ṣarī al-Dīn ʿAbd al-Barr Ibn Shihnah
bio: A prominent Hanafi scholar from Aleppo, renowned for his expertise in Islamic jurisprudence, history, and literature. He authored significant chronicles and works on the Mamluk period and was a distinguished figure in the Hanafi school of his era.

name: Ibrāhīm ibn Mūsā al-Ṭarābulusī
bio: A Hanafi jurist and scholar from Tripoli (Ṭarābulus). He contributed to the development and teaching of Hanafi fiqh in the Levant during the early 10th century AH.

name: Aḥmad ibn Sulaymān Ibn Kamāl Pāshā
bio: A distinguished Ottoman scholar and jurist who served as **Shaykh al-Islām** (the highest religious authority in the Ottoman Empire). He was a crucial figure in codifying the Ottoman iteration of Hanafi thought and authored around 200 works across Turkish, Persian, and Arabic, including a history of the Hanafi school (*Risāla fī Ṭabaqāt al-Mujtahidīn*).

name: Aḥmad ibn Yūnus Ibn al-Shilbī
bio: A Hanafi scholar active in the mid-10th century AH. He was known for his contributions to Hanafi jurisprudence, though specific biographical details in English sources are sparse.

name: Shams al-Dīn Muḥammad al-Quhustānī
bio: A notable Hanafi scholar and jurist from Khurasān/Quhistān. He authored a commentary on the *Wiqāyat al-Hidāyah* of Ṣadr al-Sharīʿah, and was recognized for his contributions to Islamic jurisprudence during this period.

name: Ibrāhīm ibn Muḥammad al-Ḥalabī
bio: Author of Multāqā al-Abḥur — the most widely-studied Ḥanafī mukhtaṣar (concise handbook) in the Ottoman Empire and beyond. It synthesised the four key pre-Ottoman Ḥanafī texts and became the basis for the Ottoman legal code (Mecelle). Also wrote Ghunyat al-Mutamallī, a ḥajj manual. Died 956 AH (1549 CE).

name: Zayn al-Dīn ibn Ibrāhīm Ibn al-Nujaym
bio: Born in Cairo circa 926 AH, Ibn al-Nujaym is the author of Al-Baḥr al-Rāʾiq — a monumental supercommentary on al-Hidāyah that rivals Fatḥ al-Qadīr in importance. His al-Ashbāh wa al-Naẓāʾir established the genre of legal maxims (qawāʿid fiqhiyyah) within the Ḥanafī tradition, a work of such importance that it spawned an entire sub-genre. He died in 970 AH (1563 CE).

name: Muḥammad ibn Bīr ʿAlī al-Birgawī
bio: Also known as Imam Birgivi, he was a Hanafi jurist, moralist, and scholar born in Balıkesir (1523). He was renowned for his staunch dedication to Islamic scholarship and social reform, authoring works on fiqh and spiritual practice that remain influential throughout the Muslim world.

name: Muḥammad al-Mawlā Abū al-Suʿūd
bio: A preeminent Ottoman scholar and jurist who served as **Shaykh al-Islām** under Sultan Suleiman the Magnificent. He is celebrated for his influential Quranic commentary (*Tafsīr Abū al-Suʿūd*) and for his role in standardizing Ottoman Hanafi jurisprudence.
note: Famous Qurʾānic exegete

name: Sinān al-Dīn Yūsuf al-Amāsī
bio: An Ottoman Hanafi scholar who held the prestigious position of **Shaykh al-Haram** in Makkah. He authored *Tabyīn al-Maḥārim*, a significant work on the prohibitions in the Quran from a juridical and ethical perspective, and taught Mullā ʿAlī al-Qārī.

name: Aḥmad ibn Maḥmūd Qāḍī Zādah
bio: A Hanafi scholar and mathematician-astronomer who served as a teacher and jurist. He contributed to the sciences of Islamic jurisprudence alongside his work in astronomy and mathematics in the Ottoman realm.

name: Raḥmat Allāh al-Sindī
bio: A noted Hanafi scholar from Sind (modern-day Pakistan) who studied hadith in the Hejaz with scholars like Ibn ʿArrāq. He taught extensively in Gujarat and Makkah, leaving a mark on Hanafi jurisprudence and hadith studies. Ḥājī Khalīfah records his death around this period.

name: Sirāj al-Dīn ʿUmar ibn Ibrāhīm Ibn Nujaym
bio: A distinguished Egyptian Hanafi jurist renowned for his work on legal maxims and analogical reasoning. He authored *Al-Ashbāh wa al-Naẓāʾir* and *Al-Nahr al-Fāʾiq*, a commentary on al-Nasafī’s *Kanz al-Daqāʾiq*, making him a central figure in Hanafi legal methodology.

name: Shihāb al-Dīn al-Tumurtāshī al-Ghazzī
bio: A Hanafi jurist from Gaza who authored *Fatāwā al-Tumurtāshī* and *Muʿīn al-Muftī*, practical works on issuing legal verdicts that became standard references in the Hanafi school.

name: ʿAlī ibn Sulṭān Mullā ʿAlī al-Qārī
bio: A Hanafi polymath from Herat who settled in Makkah, considered one of the masters of hadith and imams of fiqh, Quranic commentary, and tasawwuf in Hanafi circles. He was a *ḥāfiẓ* and famous calligrapher who authored over 100 works, most notably *Mirqāt al-Mafātīḥ*, the most extensive commentary on *Mishkāt al-Maṣābīḥ*.

name: Abū Muḥammad al-Baghdādī
bio: A Hanafi scholar active in Baghdad during the early 11th century AH. He contributed to the transmission and teaching of Hanafi jurisprudence in Iraq, though detailed biographical records in English are limited.

name: Ismāʿīl ibn ʿAbd al-Ghanī al-Nābulusī
bio: A Hanafi scholar from Nablus/Damascus. He was a jurist and teacher, part of the scholarly Nabulusi family, and contributed to the Hanafi tradition in Ottoman Syria.

name: Abū al-Ikhlāṣ Ḥasan al-Shurunbulālī
bio: A major Egyptian Hanafi *imām* and verifier (*muḥaqqiq*) whose works form an integral part of the school. He authored *Nūr al-Īḍāḥ*, one of the most widely studied primers in Hanafi fiqh, and was considered the greatest of his time in possession of *fiqh* and knowledge of legal texts.
works: Nūr al-Īḍāḥ / al-Ghunyah Ḥāshiyah ʿalā al-Durar

name: ʿAbd al-Raḥmān Shaykhī Zādah al-Dāmād
bio: An Ottoman Hanafi scholar and jurist who served as a military judge (*Qāḍī ʿAskar*) and was a prominent teacher and author in Istanbul, contributing to the codification of Ottoman Hanafi jurisprudence.

name: Khayr al-Dīn ibn Aḥmad al-Ramlī
bio: A prominent Hanafi jurist and mufti in Ottoman Palestine, born in Ramla (1585). He authored *Al-Fatāwā al-Khayriyyah*, an extensive collection of fatwas that became a cornerstone of Hanafi jurisprudence in the 18th and 19th centuries, addressing land tenure, inheritance, and social disputes.

name: ʿAbd al-Qādir ibn Yūsuf Qadrī Afandī
bio: An Ottoman Hanafi jurist and scholar who served as a teacher and mufti in the imperial administration. He contributed to Hanafi legal literature during the height of Ottoman scholarly production.

name: ʿAlāʾ al-Dīn Muḥammad al-Ḥaskafī
bio: Born in 1025 AH (1616 CE) in Ḥaskaf (near Diyarbakır), al-Ḥaskafī is the author of Al-Durr al-Mukhtār — the decisive Ḥanafī text for fatwā-giving. This concise but comprehensive manual of Ḥanafī law, paired with Ibn ʿĀbidīn's Radd al-Muḥtār commentary, forms the most authoritative legal reference in the late Ḥanafī tradition. Al-Durr al-Muntaqā is its companion work on rituals. Died 1088 AH (1677 CE).
note: Author of Al-Durr al-Mukhtār

name: Aḥmad ibn Muḥammad al-Ḥamawī
bio: A Hanafi scholar from Ḥamāh (Syria) whose contributions spanned various fields of Islamic studies, particularly remembered for his works in Hanafi jurisprudence reflecting the intellectual currents of his time.

name: Shaykh al-Islām Muḥammad al-Anqarawī
bio: An Ottoman Hanafi scholar who held the prestigious title of Shaykh al-Islām. He authored *Al-Fatāwā al-Anqarawiyyah*, a collection of legal verdicts that served as a reference for Hanafi jurists in the Ottoman Empire.

name: Ibrāhīm ibn Ḥusayn ibn Aḥmad Ibn Bīrī
bio: An Ottoman Hanafi scholar from Medina (born 1611). He was a jurist and teacher in the Hejaz, buried in Jannat al-Muʿallā, and contributed to the transmission of Hanafi fiqh in the Ottoman Arabian provinces.

name: Asʿad ibn Abī Bakr al-Uskudārī al-Madanī
bio: A Hanafi scholar from Üsküdar (Istanbul) who taught in Medina. He was a jurist and hadith scholar who contributed to the scholarly networks connecting the Ottoman center with the Hejaz.

name: ʿAbd al-Ghanī ibn Ismāʿīl al-Nābulusī
bio: A Damascene Hanafi jurist, Sufi master, and one of the most prolific authors in Islamic history with over 200 works. He synthesized theology, law, mysticism, and literature, and is renowned for his defense of Ibn ʿArabī and Sufi traditions.

name: Abū al-Suʿūd al-Azharī
bio: A Hanafi scholar associated with al-Azhar in Cairo. He contributed to the teaching and development of Hanafi jurisprudence in Egypt during the 12th century AH.

name: Abū Saʿīd Muḥammad al-Khādimī
bio: An Ottoman Hanafi-Maturidi scholar from Khadim, near Konya. He became one of the most widely read Hanafi authors of the Ottoman period, particularly known for his works on Islamic legal methodology and ethics.

name: Muḥammad Hāshim al-Tatāwī
bio: A leading Hanafi theologian from Thatta (Sindh, present-day Pakistan) who served as Imam of the Grand Mosque at Thatta. He was famous for his missionary work and contributions to Sufism and Hanafi jurisprudence in the Indian subcontinent.

name: Ibrāhīm ibn Muṣṭafā al-Madhārī al-Ḥalabī
bio: A Hanafi scholar from Aleppo who was a jurist and teacher, contributing to the continuation of Hanafi legal scholarship in Ottoman Syria.

name: Muṣṭafā ibn Muḥammad al-Tāʾī
bio: A Hanafi jurist and scholar from the Ottoman realms. He authored and taught works on Hanafi fiqh, serving as a link in the transmission of the school during the 12th century AH.

name: Aḥmad ibn Muḥammad al-Taḥtāwī
bio: A Hanafi scholar and jurist from the Tahtawi family of scholars. He was a teacher of fiqh and usul, and part of the scholarly tradition that continued to produce commentaries on major Hanafi texts.

name: Muḥammad Amīn Ibn ʿĀbidīn al-Shāmī
bio: Born in Damascus in 1198 AH (1784 CE), Ibn ʿĀbidīn is universally recognised as the preeminent Ḥanafī authority of the 19th century — and perhaps the last great systematiser of the entire madhhab. His Radd al-Muḥtār ʿalā al-Durr al-Mukhtār (commonly called Ḥāshiyat Ibn ʿĀbidīn) is the most authoritative Ḥanafī furūʿ reference in existence: a vast supercommentary that evaluates every major legal opinion and determines the acted-upon view (al-aṣaḥḥ) of the school. His al-ʿUqūd al-Durriyyah is an invaluable biographical dictionary of Ḥanafī scholars. Died 1252 AH (1836 CE).
note: The preeminent Ḥanafī authority of the 19th century

name: Muḥammad ʿĀbid al-Sindī al-Madanī
bio: A renowned Indian-origin Hanafi hadith scholar who settled in Madina. He wrote a large commentary on the *Musnad* of Imam Abū Ḥanīfah and was known for his expertise in hadith and Hanafi jurisprudence in the Hejaz.

name: ʿAbd al-Ghanī al-Ghunaymī al-Maydānī
bio: A prominent Hanafi jurist from Maydān (near Damascus). He authored *Al-Lubāb*, a celebrated commentary on *Al-Hidāyah*, and was a teacher of Ibn ʿĀbidīn, making him a pivotal figure in late Ottoman Hanafi jurisprudence.

name: Abū al-Ḥasanāt ʿAbd al-Ḥayy al-Laknawī
bio: Born in Lucknow in 1264 AH (1848 CE), al-Laknawī was one of the greatest Ḥanafī ḥadīth scholars of the modern era. His ʿUmdat al-Riʿāyah and al-Siʿāyah are essential works on ḥadīth criticism from a Ḥanafī perspective, and his contributions to establishing the reliability of Ḥanafī ḥadīth chains challenged the criticisms of anti-madhhab polemicists. He died in 1304 AH (1886 CE).
note: Major Ḥanafī ḥadīth scholar

name: Shihāb al-Dīn al-Marjānī
bio: A Tatar Hanafi scholar from Kazan, Russia, and a leading figure among the Muslim scholars of the Russian Empire. He contributed to Hanafi jurisprudence and Islamic education in the Volga region.

name: ʿAlāʾ al-Dīn Muḥammad Ibn ʿĀbidīn
bio: A Hanafi jurist and the father of the more famous Muḥammad Amīn Ibn ʿĀbidīn. He was a scholar and teacher in Damascus, part of the scholarly family that produced the famous *Radd al-Muḥtār*.

name: Muḥammad al-ʿAbbāsī al-Mahdī
bio: An Egyptian Hanafi scholar and jurist who contributed to the legal and theological discourse of his time, associated with the scholarly circles of al-Azhar and Cairo.

name: Muḥammad Kāmil al-Ṭarābulusī
bio: A Hanafi jurist from Tripoli (Lebanon) who served as a teacher and mufti. He contributed to the development of Hanafi fiqh in the Levant during the late Ottoman period.

name: Amīn ʿAlī Ḥaydar Afandī
bio: An Ottoman Hanafi jurist and scholar who served in the imperial religious administration. He authored works on fiqh and contributed to the legal education system of the empire.

name: Muḥammad Qāsim Nanāwtawī
bio: Born 1832 in Nanauta, Muhammad Qasim Nanautawi — titled Hujjat al-Islam — was the principal co-founder of the Deoband movement and Darul Uloom Deoband (1866). He studied at Delhi College under Mamluk Ali Nanautawi alongside Rashid Ahmad Gangohi, and participated in the 1857 uprising, fighting at the Battle of Shamli. After the rebellion he threw himself into institutional work — establishing Darul Uloom Deoband, building its mosque in 1868, and founding madaris across Thanabhavan, Galautti, Kerana, Danapur, Meerut, and Muradabad. His curriculum centred on the Qur'an, Hadith, Fiqh, and logic — a framework that shaped hundreds of institutions across the Muslim world. A prominent advocate of widow remarriage, he authored Aab-i Hayat and other works. Died 1880 in Deoband, aged 47.
works: Āb-i Ḥayāt (commentary on the life of the Prophet); Taḥzīr al-Nās; Mubāḥasah Shāhjahānpūr; Taṣfiyat al-Aqāʾid
note: Co-founder of Deoband; established the network of Deobandi madaris

name: Rashīd Aḥmad Gangohī
bio: A leading Indian Hanafi scholar and co-founder of the Deobandi movement and Darul Uloom Deoband. He was a master of hadith and fiqh, and his lectures on *Ṣaḥīḥ al-Bukhārī* and *Jāmiʿ al-Tirmidhī* were published as major commentaries.

name: ʿAbd al-Qādir al-Rāfiʿī
bio: A Hanafi scholar and jurist who contributed to the legal and scholarly traditions of his time, though English biographical details are sparse.

name: Muḥammad Khālid al-Atāsī
bio: A Hanafi scholar from the Atāsī family of Homs (Syria), known for their scholarly contributions. He was a jurist and teacher in the Levant.

name: ʿAzīz al-Raḥmān ʿUthmānī
bio: The first Head Mufti of Darul Uloom Deoband and an Indian Hanafi jurist. He authored *Fatāwā Dārul ʿUlūm Deoband* (in 12 volumes), issuing over 37,000 fatwas, and was a key figure in institutionalizing Deobandi Hanafi jurisprudence.

name: Khalīl ibn ʿAbd al-Qādir al-Nahlawī
bio: A Hanafi scholar from the Nahlawi family, known for their contributions to Hanafi fiqh. He was a jurist and teacher in the late Ottoman or early modern period.

name: Muḥammad Bakhīt al-Muṭīʿī
bio: A leading Egyptian Hanafi-Maturidi scholar who served as **Grand Mufti of Egypt** (1914–1921) and rector of al-Azhar. He was a prolific author known for his conservative stance on Islamic reform and his work *Tathīr al-Fuʾād*.

name: Aḥmad ibn Muḥammad Zarqā
bio: A Syrian Hanafi scholar and jurist. He was a teacher of ʿAbd al-Fattāḥ Abū Ghuddah and contributed to the tradition of Hanafi legal scholarship in Syria.

name: Ashraf ʿAlī Thānawī
bio: Born 1863 in Thana Bhawan, Ashraf Ali Thanwi — known as Hakimul Ummat (Physician of the Ummah) — was one of the most influential Deobandi scholars of the modern era. A graduate of Darul Uloom Deoband (1883), he taught at Madrasa Faiz-e-Aam in Kanpur for 14 years before returning to revive the Khanqah-i-Imdadiyah in Thana Bhawan, where he founded Madrasa Ashrafiya. He authored over a thousand works including Bayan al-Qur'an, Bahishti Zewar (his bestselling manual of Islamic conduct for women), and Imdad al-Fatawa. He was a leading supporter of the Pakistan Movement and corresponded directly with Muhammad Ali Jinnah. Muhammad Iqbal regarded him as the greatest living authority on Rumi. He trained approximately 1,000 disciples and remained a towering figure of South Asian Sunni orthodoxy until his death in 1943.
works: Bayān al-Qurʾān, Bahishtī Zewar, Imdād al-Fatāwā, Nashr al-Ṭibb fī Dhikr al-Nabī al-Ḥabīb
note: Hakimul Ummat — author of over 1,000 works; leading supporter of the Pakistan Movement

name: Kifāyatullāh Dehlawī
bio: An Indian Hanafi jurist and the second President of Jamiat Ulama-e-Hind. He served as the second rector of Madrasa Aminia and was regarded as the **Grand Mufti of India**. He was a founding member of Jamia Millia Islamia and authored *Kifāyat al-Muftī*.

name: Aḥmad ibn Muḥammad al-Kurdī
bio: A Hanafi scholar from the Kurdish regions who contributed to the transmission of Hanafi jurisprudence in his area.

name: Zafar Aḥmad ʿUthmānī
bio: A prominent Deobandi Hanafi jurist and hadith scholar, nephew of Ashraf ʿAlī Thānawī. He authored **Iʿlāʾ al-Sunan**, an 18-volume encyclopedia of hadith proofs for the Hanafi school, and was a key figure in the Pakistan Movement.

name: Muḥammad Shafīʿ Deobandī
bio: A leading Pakistani Deobandi Hanafi scholar, former Grand Mufti of Pakistan, and founder of **Darul Uloom Karachi** (1951). He was a master of tafsīr, hadith, and fiqh, and his magnum opus *Maʿārif al-Qurʾān* is one of the most widely read Urdu Quranic commentaries.

name: Maḥmūd Ḥasan Gangohī
bio: A senior Indian Deobandi Hanafi scholar who served as **Grand Mufti of Darul Uloom Deoband** and **Mazahir al-ʿUlūm**. He was a top deputy (*khalīfah*) of Shaykh Zakariyyā Kandhlawī and one of the most respected jurists of his era.

name: Niẓām al-Dīn Aʿẓamī
bio: An Indian Deobandi Hanafi scholar and hadith expert who taught at Darul Uloom Deoband and was known for his expertise in hadith and Hanafi jurisprudence.

name: ʿAbd al-Raḥīm Lajpūrī
bio: A South African Deobandi Hanafi scholar of Indian origin. He was a prominent jurist and author, known for his *Fatāwā Raḥīmiyya* and for establishing the first Darul Uloom in South Africa (Azaadville).

name: Rashīd Aḥmad Ludhyānwī
bio: A Pakistani Deobandi Hanafi scholar and author of *Aḥsan al-Fatāwā*. He was a senior jurist and mufti known for his detailed legal rulings and was a leading scholar of the Ahl-e-Sunnat tradition in Pakistan.

name: Abū ʿAlī al-Shāshī
bio: A Hanafi jurist from Shāsh (near modern Tashkent), known for his contributions to the principles of Hanafi jurisprudence. His work *Uṣūl al-Shāshī* was a significant text in the development of legal theory within the school. He was an important figure in the Central Asian Hanafi tradition.
note: Wrote one of the earliest systematic Ḥanafī uṣūl al-fiqh treatises; foundational text cited by al-Jaṣṣāṣ, al-Bazdawī, and al-Sarakhsī

name: Abū al-Muʾīn al-Nasafī
bio: A preeminent Hanafi-Māturīdī theologian from Nasaf, considered the most important figure in the Māturīdī school after Imām al-Māturīdī himself. He authored *Tabṣirat al-Adilla* and *Bahr al-Kalām*, monumental works in Islamic theology that systematized Māturīdī doctrine. He died in 508 AH, bridging the 5th and 6th centuries.
note: Principal defender of Māturīdī theology; central to the Ḥanafī theological-juristic tradition

name: Abū al-Yusr al-Bazdawī
bio: A prominent Hanafi-Māturīdī scholar and qāḍī in Samarqand, known as
note: Major Ḥanafī theologian from the Bazdawī family; systematized Ḥanafī kalām methodology

name: Saʿd al-Dīn al-Taftazānī
bio: Born in 722 AH (1322 CE) in Taftazān (near Nishapur), al-Taftazānī was a master of both uṣūl al-fiqh and kalām whose Sharḥ al-ʿAqāʾid al-Nasafiyyah is the standard commentary on al-Nasafī's creed text, studied in madrasas worldwide. His al-Talwīḥ (uṣūl) and al-Mutawwal (rhetoric) are equally foundational. He represents the synthesis of Ḥanafī jurisprudence with rigorous theological method. Died 792 AH (1390 CE).
note: Influential uṣūl al-fiqh and kalām authority; works are standard in Ḥanafī madrasa curricula worldwide

name: Shams al-Dīn al-Fanārī (Mollā Fenārī)
bio: Born in 751 AH (1350 CE) in Fenār, near Bursa, Mollā Fenārī was the first Shaykh al-Islām of the Ottoman Empire, a position he held under Murād I and Bāyezīd I. His Fuṣūl al-Badāyiʿ fī Uṣūl al-Dīn is a major work of Māturīdī theology, and his ʿAyn al-Aykām is an important uṣūl text. He institutionalised Ḥanafī fiqh as the official state madhhab of the Ottoman Empire. Died 834 AH (1431 CE).
works: Fuṣūl al-Badāyiʿ fī Uṣūl al-Dīn, ʿAyn al-Aykām
note: First Shaykh al-Islām of the Ottoman Empire; institutionalised Ḥanafī fiqh as the state madhhab

name: Khwājazādah
bio: Ottoman Hanafi scholar and jurist of the late 9th century. He was part of the scholarly establishment that shaped early Ottoman Hanafi jurisprudence.
note: Leading Ottoman Ḥanafī commentator; glosses on al-Taftazānī became a standard reference

name: Aḥmad Sirhindī (Mujaddid al-Alf al-Thānī)
bio: Born in 971 AH (1564 CE) in Sirhind, Imām Rabbānī Aḥmad Sirhindī is known as the Reviver of the Second Millennium (Mujaddid al-Alf al-Thānī). His Maktūbāt — a three-volume collection of 536 letters — addresses fiqh, theology, and spirituality, and represents one of the most intellectually influential works in South Asian Islam. He reformed the Naqshbandī Sufi order and was a staunch defender of Ḥanafī jurisprudence and Sunnī orthodoxy against syncretism. Died 1034 AH (1624 CE).
note: Reviver of the second millennium; Ḥanafī jurist whose Maktūbāt address fiqh, theology, and spirituality

name: Shāh Walī Allāh al-Dihlawī
bio: Born in Delhi in 1114 AH (1703 CE), Shāh Walī Allāh is regarded as the most important South Asian Islamic scholar of the 18th century. His Ḥujjat Allāh al-Bālighah attempted a comprehensive re-systematisation of Islamic law, drawing on Ḥanafī fiqh, Shāfiʿī methodology, and independent ijtihād. His al-Muṣaffā is a work of uṣūl al-fiqh. He translated the Qurʾān into Persian and his intellectual legacy shaped the entire subsequent trajectory of South Asian Islam. Died 1176 AH (1762 CE).
note: Most important South Asian Islamic scholar of the 18th century; re-systematised Ḥanafī law with independent ijtihād

name: Qāḍī Thanāʾ Allāh Pānīpatī
bio: A major Indian Ḥanafī scholar and exegete from Panipat, born in 1731. He authored the renowned Tafsīr al-Mazharī, a celebrated Quranic commentary that integrates fiqh rulings with tafsīr. He was a direct descendant of ʿUthmān ibn ʿAffān and served as a judge (qāḍī) in his region. Died 1225 AH (1810 CE).
works: Mawāhib al-Raḥmān, al-Kifāyah fī Sharḥ al-Hidāyah
note: Major South Asian Ḥanafī jurist and exegete; integrated fiqh rulings into tafsīr

name: Mawlānā Amjad ʿAlī Aʿẓamī
bio: Born circa 1300 AH (1882 CE) in Bahraich, Mawlānā Amjad ʿAlī was a leading Barelwī Ḥanafī scholar. His Bahār-e-Sharīʿat — a 24-volume Urdu compendium of Ḥanafī fiqh — is the most comprehensive such work in Urdu and a pillar of the Barelwī Ḥanafī tradition. It covers every area of law from purification to inheritance. Died 1367 AH (1948 CE).
note: Wrote the most comprehensive Urdu Ḥanafī fiqh compendium; pillar of the Barelwī Ḥanafī tradition

name: Muftī Taqī ʿUthmānī
bio: Born in 1363 AH (1944 CE) in Deoband, Muftī Taqī ʿUthmānī is the most prominent living Ḥanafī jurist. A member of the Islamic Fiqh Academy (OIC), he pioneered modern Islamic finance jurisprudence with works like Buhūth fī Qadāyā Fiqhiyyah Muʿāṣirah. His Takmelat al-Hidāyah continues the commentary tradition on al-Hidāyah. He served as judge on the Federal Shariat Court of Pakistan and his legal opinions carry weight across the entire Ḥanafī world.
works: Buhūth fī Qadāyā Fiqhiyyah Muʿāṣirah, Takmelat al-Hidāyah
note: Most prominent living Ḥanafī jurist; Islamic Fiqh Academy member; pioneered Islamic finance jurisprudence

name: Muḥammad Zāhid al-Kawtharī
bio: Born in 1296 AH (1879 CE) in the Ottoman Empire, al-Kawtharī was the last holder of the title Shaykh al-Islām. After the fall of the caliphate he lived in exile in Cairo, where he became the most formidable defender of the Ḥanafī madhhab and Māturīdī theology of the 20th century. His Ḥāshiyat al-ʿUqūd al-Durriyyah supercommentary and his Maqālāt collection of essays are essential reading. He was a prolific editor of classical manuscripts and a trenchant polemicist against both Wahhābī and modernist critiques. Died 1371 AH (1952 CE).
note: Shaykh al-Islām of the Ottoman Empire (last holder); towering Ḥanafī scholar, muḥaddith, and polemicist; defender of the madhhab against reformist critiques

name: ʿAbd al-Fattāḥ Abū Ghuddah
bio: A Syrian Hanafi scholar, hadith expert, and former head of the Syrian Muslim Brotherhood. He was renowned for editing and publishing classical manuscripts, his biographical works, and his teaching of hadith and fiqh. He traveled extensively to India and Pakistan, connecting Arab and South Asian scholarly traditions.
works: al-Ḥiwār fī ʿUlūm al-Ḥadīth, al-Nukat ʿalā Ibn al-Ṣalāḥ
note: Pre-eminent Syrian Ḥanafī muḥaddith and faqīh; student of al-Kawtharī; prolific editor of classical Ḥanafī and ḥadīth manuscripts; teacher of Muftī Taqī ʿUthmānī

name: Aḥmad Riḍā Khān Barelwī
bio: Born in 1272 AH (1856 CE) in Bareilly, Aḥmad Riḍā Khān was the founder of the Barelwī movement and one of the most prolific Ḥanafī scholars of the modern era. His Kanz al-Īmān is a widely-used Urdu Qurʾān translation, and his 12-volume Fatāwā Riḍawiyyah addresses every conceivable legal question. He defended traditional Sunnī practices against reformist critiques and wrote extensively in fiqh, ʿaqīdah, and ḥadīth. Died 1340 AH (1921 CE).
works: Ḥasanayn al-ʿUqūd al-Durriyyah fī Tarjamat al-Ṣiddīqah, Kanz al-Īmān (Qurʾān translation), Fatāwā Riḍawiyyah (12 vols.)
note: Founder of the Barelwī movement; prolific Ḥanafī jurist, muḥaddith, and polemicist who defended traditional Sunni practices; wrote across fiqh, ʿaqīdah, and ḥadīth

name: Akhtar Riḍā Khān
bio: A prominent Indian Barelvi Hanafi scholar, grandson of Ahmad Rida Khan. He was a jurist and spiritual guide who continued the family tradition of issuing fatwas and leading the Barelvi movement in India.
works: Fatāwā Riḍawiyyah (new ed.), Dār al-Iftāʾ al-ʿĀlamiyyah rulings
note: Grand Muftī of the Barelwī Ḥanafī tradition; great-grandson of Aḥmad Riḍā Khān; headed Dār al-Iftāʾ and issued extensive fatāwā continuing the Barelwī jurisprudential legacy
